text:
Different objective lenses (e.g., 10X, 40X) to achieve better magnification and resolution under visible light. Explore different parts of a microscope (Fig. 2.2) in your school laboratory and use it to observe fine structures of various materials. Under the microscope, you will see a magnified image of an object. Can you estimate its actual size?

Activity 2.1: Let us estimate the size of a cell
1. Take a transparent ruler with millimetre (mm) markings.
2. Place the ruler on the stage of the microscope, focus on it using the adjustment knob and observe the diameter of the circular field of view through the eyepiece and measure it in mm.
3. Convert the diameter from mm to micrometre (µm). Suppose the diameter of the visible field is 5 mm, meaning 5 × 1000 = 5000 µm.
4. Remove the ruler and place an onion peel slide on the stage of the microscope.
5. Focus on the slide and count the number of cells present along the diameter of the field of view in one straight line.
6. Estimate the real size of the cell using the formula:

Estimated size of the onion peel cell = 

Unit conversion:
1 millimetre (mm) = 1000 micrometre (µm)

[Image showing calculation: 5000 µm / 5 cells = 1000 µm/cell = 1 mm/cell]

(The calculation box shows: 5000 µm / 5 cells = 1000 µm/cell = 1 mm/cell)

Thus, a microscope allows us to see very small structures clearly and is an essential tool for studying the cell structure. diagrams.label: Structure of a light microscope
diagrams.description: A labeled diagram illustrating the structure of a light microscope. The components are arranged vertically. From the bottom up, the parts include the Base, the Arm, the Stage (where the slide rests), the Objective lens (the lens closest to the sample), the Coarse adjustment knob (used for initial focusing), and the Eyepiece (the lens viewed by the observer).

diagrams.label: An electron microscope
diagrams.description: A diagram showing the complex structure of an electron microscope. It depicts a large, sophisticated apparatus designed for viewing extremely small objects. The overall structure suggests multiple internal components necessary for generating and detecting electron beams.
